missing_angles.angle: The deflationary AI-capex bust / stranded-asset scenario. Part 2 commits entirely to a persistent-bottleneck + inflation + financial-repression regime and tells the reader to 'own duration into financial repression' (F8). But the notes carry an equally-developed opposite tail: fiber-overbuild overshoot, Fisher debt-deflation from a capex bust, GPU-as-collateral margin-call cascades, circular vendor financing, and a Perez installation-phase crash.
missing_angles.source_notes: T1 cru#5 (self-liquidating vs stranded overhang), T1 hyp#34 (Fisher debt-deflation inverting the dominant scenario), T1 hyp#68 ('double whammy' revenue collapse + spending surge), T1 cru#70 (in an AI bust do Treasuries rally or fall?), T6 evi#8 (fiber overbuild), T6 hyp#36 (GPU-collateral margin-call cascade), T6 the#35 (circular vendor financing), T3 the#56 (Perez bubble→crash).
missing_angles.materiality: This directly inverts the headline bet. If the cycle is a bust rather than a bottleneck, negative real rates (F8) and 'Treasuries as funding haven' (F15) may not hold — T1 cru#70 explicitly asks whether Treasuries rally or fall with equities in a fiscal-credibility break. A Bridgewater judge will notice the framework only prices one side of a two-sided crux and never states the disconfirming scenario.
missing_angles.suggested_fix: Add a paragraph naming the bust tail as the primary alternative regime, and hedge the F8/duration bet with the condition under which it flips (correlated AI-equity/fiscal-revenue shock → Treasuries sell off, not rally).

missing_angles.angle: Algorithmic-efficiency collapse of compute demand (the DeepSeek shock). Part 2's entire thesis is that electrons/fabs are the binding constraint (D1/D3). The notes repeatedly argue efficiency gains can collapse training-compute demand and ease the physical bottleneck without any buildout — the exact mechanism that would relieve the constraint the framework treats as immovable.
missing_angles.source_notes: T2 evi#3 (DeepSeek V3/R1 at <10% expected compute), T5 evi#3 / T5 hyp#9 (compute-efficient training undermines hardware denial), T6 evi#13 (algorithmic efficiency may collapse training-compute demand before buildout completes), T6 evi#93 (~$6M R1 training cost), T9 evi#95 (controls incentivize exactly the efficiency research that routes around them).
missing_angles.materiality: If efficiency, not grid, becomes binding, F1's data-center slip is caused by demand softening rather than grid gating — undercutting the 'watch the grid, not the leaderboard' claim. It also weakens the mercantilist-trap logic: you can't will electrons into existence, but you can route around needing them. This is the single most-cited counter to the core thesis and it is absent.
missing_angles.suggested_fix: Address the efficiency counter head-on: argue why physical constraints still bind at the deployment/inference frontier even as per-token training cost falls (Jevons rebound), or concede it as a downside risk to D3.

missing_angles.angle: The China power-and-nuclear asymmetry. Part 2's binding-constraint story is implicitly US-centric ('at least one US 1 GW data center slips'), but the notes document that China is building generation at a pace that makes it far less grid-constrained than the US.
missing_angles.source_notes: T5 evi#56 (China ~150 GW/yr generation buildout), T5 evi#57 (China ~27 reactors under construction vs 10+ yr US nuclear licensing), T6 the#6 / T5 the#10 (two parallel stacks with incompatible economics), T5 cru#54 (power displaces chips as binding constraint ~2028).
missing_angles.materiality: If the electron constraint binds the US but not China, then 'electrons gate everyone equally, so no one gets a decisive lead' is wrong — the constraint is asymmetric and could hand China a buildout lead. This weakens both the 'no decisive lead' conclusion and the symmetry assumption behind the prisoner's dilemma.
missing_angles.suggested_fix: Explicitly compare US vs China grid/nuclear buildout rates and state why the asymmetry does or does not produce a durable Chinese compute advantage; adjust the 'no decisive lead' claim accordingly.

missing_angles.angle: Talent as the actually-binding constraint. The title asserts the constraint is 'not intelligence,' yet the notes make a strong, repeated case that scarce, super-linearly-concentrated frontier talent — gated by immigration policy — is a harder bottleneck than capital or power. Immigration-as-de-facto-industrial-policy appears nowhere in Part 2.
missing_angles.source_notes: T6 the#81 (PhD-pipeline inelasticity harder than capital/power), T6 the#82 (power-law researcher concentration), T6 hyp#83 (H-1B/green-card backlog as de facto AI policy), T6 evi#85 (return migration of Chinese-origin researchers), T3 cru#51 (talent vs compute binding-constraint crux), T5 evi#25 (visa restrictions concentrate Chinese talent at home).
missing_angles.materiality: This is a direct alternative to the framework's central claim. If talent is co-binding, then jurisdiction matters through immigration law (a lever the framework never names) and 'own power and platforms' is an incomplete bet. A judge sees the framework asserting 'not intelligence' without engaging the best counter to it.
missing_angles.suggested_fix: Add talent/immigration as a third physical-scarcity axis alongside electrons and silicon, or explicitly argue why it is subordinate; consider an immigration-policy watch indicator.

missing_angles.angle: The Gulf / sovereign price-insensitive capital as a third pole. Part 2 collapses the world into 'two loosely-coupled compute stacks.' The notes give a whole topic (T8) to Gulf sovereign capital as price-insensitive buyers who extend the capex cycle beyond market discipline and constitute a possible third node that arbitrages the bifurcation.
missing_angles.source_notes: T1 the#21 (sovereign AI funds as price-insensitive buyers extending capex), T6 hyp#18 (Gulf capital accepting sub-market returns distorts economics), T8 the#2/#35/#42 (rentier capital buys pole status; captures rent from bifurcation itself), T8 the#36 (near-zero marginal energy cost = inference price floor).
missing_angles.materiality: Price-insensitive sovereign capital undercuts the 'capex prisoner's dilemma raises r*' mechanism (these buyers don't bid competitively on the same rate-sensitive basis) and undercuts the two-stack framing that F12 rests on. It also bears on F1: sovereign anchor tenants can finance through a grid slip. Its absence makes the r* and bifurcation claims cleaner than the evidence supports.
missing_angles.suggested_fix: Introduce Gulf/sovereign capital as a distinct actor that both extends the capex wave (dampening the self-correcting bust) and creates a neutral third node between the two stacks; note its effect on r* attribution.

missing_angles.angle: Catastrophe-triggered backlash and the liability/insurance adoption brake. Part 2 frames backlash as reactive to labor displacement, arriving 'after bargaining power has fragmented' (D4→D5, F7). The notes argue the dominant backlash trigger is a single high-salience agentic failure — and that the 'safety/accountability' frame beats the 'jobs' frame — with insurance/liability acting as an adoption ceiling independent of displacement.
missing_angles.source_notes: T3 the#83 (one-catastrophe tipping point, backlash need not await aggregate displacement), T3 hyp#91 ('safety & accountability' frame outcompetes 'jobs' frame), T3 the#80 (actuarial unquantifiability → insurance adoption ceiling), T3 cru#88 (timing of first catastrophic failure vs liability codification), T9 hyp#6 (clampdown triggered by single event), T7 the#42 (insurers as de facto standards body).
missing_angles.materiality: This changes the timing and channel of F7 and the backlash narrative: backlash could arrive early and via safety/liability, not late and via labor politics. It also introduces an adoption brake that could slow the very diffusion driving displacement — a two-way effect the framework doesn't model.
missing_angles.suggested_fix: Reframe the 'fear' section to include catastrophe/liability as the faster, non-labor backlash pathway, and note insurance/tort as a diffusion brake that could push F7 timing and magnitude either way.

missing_angles.angle: Demographics as the primary driver of both r* and displacement absorption. Part 2 attributes r* elevation solely to the mercantilist capex dilemma and treats displacement as unabsorbed. The notes argue demographics — not AI capex — may drive the savings-glut reversal, and that shrinking labor forces could absorb displacement and mute the backlash.
missing_angles.source_notes: T1 the#57 (demographics, not AI capex, drives savings-glut reversal), T1 cru#63 (does demographic contraction absorb AI displacement or does displacement outrun it?), T3 evi#16 (aging-economy labor shortages reduce displacement pressure), T1 evi#28 (industrial policy can crowd IN private capital, undermining r* elevation).
missing_angles.materiality: If demographics drive r*, the causal story behind F8 is misattributed and the policy lever changes. If labor-force contraction absorbs displacement, F7 (white-collar employment falls) is weakened. Both are load-bearing forecasts resting on a single mechanism where the notes offer a competing one.
missing_angles.suggested_fix: Acknowledge demographics as a co-driver of r* and as a partial offset to displacement; state why the capex mechanism dominates on the relevant horizon.

missing_angles.angle: Durable software/middleware and equipment chokepoints (CUDA, ASML/EUV, EDA). Part 2 says 'no durable model moat forms' and controls are 'aimed at the wrong layer' because value migrated up-stack. But the notes identify durable, geography-independent moats and tighter chokepoints the framework skips: CUDA (10+ yr to replicate), ASML EUV, and EDA.
missing_angles.source_notes: T5 evi#19 (CUDA durable 10+ yr moat), T6 the#27 (CUDA geography-independent moat), T9 evi#25 / T5 evi#35 (ASML EUV a tighter chokepoint than TSMC), T5 evi#90 (EDA — Cadence/Synopsys/Siemens — upstream chokepoint), T2 evi#16 (proprietary RLHF/feedback moat).
missing_angles.materiality: These undercut two claims at once: 'no durable moat' (CUDA and RLHF data are durable) and 'controls aimed at the wrong layer' (ASML/EUV and EDA are precisely the layers that still bind and don't leak like finished chips). The 'chokepoint with an expiry date' verdict is too strong if the binding chokepoint is EUV/EDA, not GPUs.
missing_angles.suggested_fix: Distinguish the leaky finished-chip chokepoint from the durable EUV/EDA/CUDA layer; concede that at least one Western chokepoint (equipment/toolchain) has a longer expiry than the framework implies.

missing_angles.angle: The actual mechanism of swing-state alignment (standards, Digital Silk Road, language, 'good enough'). F12 asserts swing states adopt Chinese stacks via 'supply-chain weaponization and financing,' but the notes locate the real levers in standards capture, developer-toolchain lock-in, Digital Silk Road financing, language-data advantage, and a 'good-enough' threshold that makes the frontier gap irrelevant.
missing_angles.source_notes: T5 the#77 ('good enough' threshold irrelevant to Global South), T5 evi#78 (Huawei 5G capture precedent), T5 evi#82 (ITU-T/ISO standards embedding), T5 the#80/#81 (Digital Silk Road financing; toolchain first-mover network effects), T5 hyp#85 (language-capability advantage), T8 evi#59 (DSR in 100+ countries).
missing_angles.materiality: F12's probability and its resolution criteria depend on which mechanism dominates. If standards/toolchain lock-in and language are decisive, alignment can occur even where financing is neutral — and the 'watch supply-chain financing, not model quality' guidance is only half right. The causal claim behind a live forecast is underspecified.
missing_angles.suggested_fix: Replace 'supply-chain weaponization and financing' with the fuller mechanism set (standards + toolchain lock-in + DSR finance + language + 'good enough'), and pick the leading indicator that best predicts F12.

missing_angles.angle: Taiwan seizure-vs-destruction distinction and Mutual Assured Production Destruction. Part 2 treats F11 as a monolithic 'Taiwan tail' priced at 0.12. The notes contain the analysis that actually governs that probability: seizure (not destruction) is the coherent Chinese strategy, seized fabs may be inoperable without foreign inputs, and MAPD makes China itself hostage — plus an inventory-cliff dynamic that makes the first 12–18 months far worse than equilibrium models.
missing_angles.source_notes: T9 evi#10 (seizure not destruction is coherent), T9 evi#13 (seized fabs need continuous foreign inputs), T5 the#36 (MAPD — China symmetrically hostage), T9 the#36 (inventory-cliff makes first 12–18 months catastrophic), T9 evi#40 (pre-conflict stockpiling decouples capability from the moment).
missing_angles.materiality: The deterrence logic (MAPD, non-functional seized fabs) is what justifies a low F11 probability and is also what would size the economic impact if it happened. Stating only 'interdependence does not price it out' omits the mechanism a judge would want to see and leaves the 0.12 unjustified and the impact-magnitude un-scoped.
missing_angles.suggested_fix: Add one or two sentences on MAPD and the seizure/inoperability logic to justify the low probability, and note the inventory-cliff as the near-term severity driver if the tail fires.

missing_angles.angle: Enterprise ROI disappointment / adoption-lag as a demand-side brake on both buildout and displacement. Part 2 assumes the buildout continues (gated only by physics) and that displacement proceeds. The notes flag a demand-side risk: enterprise 'good enough' plateaus and ROI disappointment could trigger investment withdrawal before displacement reaches systemic scale.
missing_angles.source_notes: T3 evi#9 (enterprise adoption lag systematically underestimated), T3 hyp#60 (ROI disappointment triggers investment withdrawal before labor substitution), T6 hyp#94 ('good enough' demand asymptote), T2 evi#30 (enterprise capability plateau decouples frontier scaling from business value).
missing_angles.materiality: A demand-side stall is a distinct path to F1 (data-center cancellations from soft demand, not grid) and a headwind to F7 (displacement slower than assumed). It also feeds the bust scenario. The framework's 'watch the grid, not the leaderboard' misses that watching enterprise ROI could be the earlier signal for a capex reversal.
missing_angles.suggested_fix: Add enterprise ROI/adoption as a second leading indicator alongside grid metrics, and note that a demand-side stall is an alternative (non-physical) cause of the F1 data-center slip.

missing_angles.angle: Rearmament and the defense/IC demand floor. Part 2 builds the fiscal/r* story from AI capex + industrial policy only. The notes add rearmament as a co-equal fiscal supercycle with no productivity offset, plus a price-insensitive defense-AI demand floor that de-risks the buildout.
missing_angles.source_notes: T1 the#79 (rearmament co-equal fiscal supercycle, no productivity offset), T1 the#84 / T6 evi#29 (defense-AI off-balance-sheet demand floor), T6 the#54 (compute-as-deterrence → unbounded state demand), T3 the#77 (state-as-deployer demand floor).
missing_angles.materiality: A defense demand floor makes F1 cancellations less likely (government backstops the anchor tenant) and strengthens the r*/repression case independently of AI. Omitting rearmament understates the fiscal driver and overstates how much of the pressure is attributable to the AI-mercantilism loop specifically.
missing_angles.suggested_fix: Fold rearmament into the fiscal-supercycle argument and note the defense/IC demand floor as a force partially offsetting F1's grid-driven cancellation pressure.

top_critiques.attack: The load-bearing conclusion is asserted, not argued. The only mechanism offered for 'no decisive lead' is commoditization (D2, F2, F10): base models commoditize, rent migrates up-stack, no weights moat. But commoditization of PRICE/RENT is a categorically different claim from NO DECISIVE CAPABILITY LEAD. A lab crossing a recursive-self-improvement or agentic threshold (T9 the#3 'nuclear moment', cru#8, cru#39; T2 hyp#2 test-time compute) could hold a militarily/economically decisive lead for the window that matters even if API prices collapse the year after. The essay declares 'who wins AGI' the wrong question without engaging the takeoff literature it itself catalogues. The tell is calibration: it prices the Taiwan tail (F11=0.12) but assigns NO probability to the decisive-lead tail — the single greatest point of failure for the entire thesis is left unforecast. That asymmetry is not neutral; it hides the thesis's biggest vulnerability.
top_critiques.target: Core thesis conclusion — 'a decisive lead denied to everyone'; 'Powerful GPT, not a singularity'. Also a forecast-completeness/calibration failure.
top_critiques.best_rebuttal: Base-rate diffusion is strong: major military-relevant tech diffuses in 5–10 years (T9 evi#4), a leader's revealed architecture accelerates followers (T9 evi#28), and strategic analysis systematically over-predicts discontinuities (T9 the#78). Compute concentration is a verification enabler, not a lock (T9 the#70). Empirically, API deflation and open-weight replication (T2 evi#23, T6 evi#99) show capability leads bleeding out fast. So 'no durable decisive lead' is the higher-probability base case.
top_critiques.remaining_vulnerability: Diffusion base rates describe DEPLOYED capability, not the EXPLOITATION WINDOW. A 6–18 month lead through a takeoff threshold can be decisive before diffusion catches up — 'eventually commoditizes' is fully compatible with a decisive transient. The thesis has no forecast bounding that window, so the rebuttal answers a question ('is the lead permanent?') the attack didn't ask ('is there a decisive transient?').

top_critiques.attack: The macro spine hides a tension it treats as complementary. Section 1 says capex competition 'lifts the equilibrium real rate — capital is bid for physical scarcity.' The same section and F8 say expect SUSTAINED NEGATIVE real rates via repression. For both to hold, CPI must persistently outrun a policy rate that a rising r* is pushing UP — a narrow, unstable configuration asserted as 'the debt-management instrument, not an accident.' Yet F8 is only P=0.55 — a coin flip — and the flagship trade 'Own duration into financial repression' is built directly on that coin flip: the recommendation carries far more conviction than the forecast underneath it. Meanwhile the notes supply hard disconfirmers the essay never prices: industrial policy crowds IN private capital, undercutting r* elevation (T1 evi#28); mega-cap FCF self-funding severs rate transmission (T1 evi#33, T6 evi#41); historic tech waves are net deflationary (T1 evi#4); an AI capex bust triggers Fisher debt-deflation (T1 hyp#34).
top_critiques.target: 'The Mercantilist Capex Trap' macro mechanism; F8 (P=0.55); the headline 'Own duration into financial repression' bet.
top_critiques.best_rebuttal: The two claims are causally linked, not contradictory: high r* is precisely WHY the state must repress — it cannot grow out of debt fast enough (temporal mismatch, T1 cru#14, hyp#16), so it forces rates below inflation. WWII and Japan/YCC provide repression precedents (T1 evi#9, evi#15). P=0.55 honestly reflects the narrow technical bar (six consecutive months on a specific CPI measure).
top_critiques.remaining_vulnerability: A P=0.55 forecast cannot underwrite a high-conviction 'own duration' call — the bet is miscalibrated to its own evidence. The 'high r* → must repress' chain also requires the Fed to lose independence (T1 cru#11), which is unforecast, and the deflationary tail (evi#4, hyp#34) — which would invert the duration trade — is never assigned a probability at all.

top_critiques.attack: The containment-failure argument hinges on space/off-grid compute making 'compute stop needing a jurisdiction.' But F4 only measures a 200 MW off-grid POWER deployment — and behind-the-meter gas in Texas is 'off-grid' while sitting fully inside US jurisdiction and BIS reach. The essay conflates 'off the constrained GRID' with 'off the regulable MAP.' Off-grid power eases the ELECTRON bottleneck; it does nothing to the JURISDICTION bottleneck. Genuinely jurisdiction-agnostic orbital frontier compute is a decade-plus away on thermal, latency, and launch-mass grounds. So even if F4 resolves TRUE, it validates only the electron half of the thesis, not the containment-expiry half — yet the section sells space-compute as 'the wildcard the consensus does not price,' i.e., the key insight. The forecast can't carry the rhetoric attached to it.
top_critiques.target: 'The Consensus Is Fighting the Last Layer'; F4 (P=0.62); the 'containment has an expiry date' claim.
top_critiques.best_rebuttal: Containment leaks through many vectors independent of space-compute: chip smuggling (F5, P=0.65), third-country hubs (T5 evi#29), FDPR/cloud-API verification gaps (T5 cru#43, cru#53). The expiry-date claim survives on those alone; F4 is one node, not the whole argument.
top_critiques.remaining_vulnerability: That rebuttal demotes space-compute to decorative — but it was marketed as the unpriced core insight. Either it is load-bearing (and the off-grid ≠ jurisdiction-agnostic gap bites) or it isn't (and the section oversells a wildcard to manufacture novelty). The essay can't have it both ways.

top_critiques.attack: The flagship confirmations are near-unfalsifiable and therefore non-probative. F1 — 'at least one' 1 GW+ project 'delayed, downsized, or cancelled' — is satisfied by ordinary project churn in ANY large capex wave; it cannot discriminate between 'the grid is THE binding constraint' and routine slippage, so it lends ~zero evidential weight to the thesis it is explicitly cited to prove ('the grid, not the GPU order book, is binding'). F2 is the opposite failure: a 90% API price drop is UNDER-calibrated against the essay's own cited base rate of ~99% declines over two years (T2 evi#23, T3 evi#36) — it should sit near 0.97, not 0.88. Pricing it at 0.88 signals the numbers aren't tightly derived from the evidence base. The 'at-least-one' template (F5, F7, F12) recurs precisely because it inflates the apparent hit-rate while hollowing out informativeness.
top_critiques.target: Forecast methodology and calibration (F1 P=0.87, F2 P=0.88; recurring 'at-least-one' framing in F5, F7, F12).
top_critiques.best_rebuttal: The thresholds are deliberately conservative to be cleanly adjudicable; the thesis rests on the MECHANISM (interconnection queues, transformer lead times as leading indicators — 'watch the grid, not the leaderboard'), with F1 illustrative rather than dispositive. F2's 0.88 leaves headroom for a capability-definition dispute at resolution ('GPT-4-class' is contestable).
top_critiques.remaining_vulnerability: If F1 is merely illustrative it should not be quoted at P=0.87 as the evidence that 'the grid is binding' — but the prose does exactly that. A skeptic scores the point cleanly: the thesis's headline datapoint is a forecast that essentially cannot fail, which means it confirms nothing.

top_critiques.attack: The mercantilism brief is handled asymmetrically. The essay builds a US-centric inflationary/repression narrative but underweights the opposite-signed structural force sitting in its own notes: China's manufacturing and AI-hardware overcapacity as an ACTIVE deflation-export weapon (T1 evi#36; the#41 'inflationary-West / deflationary-East divergence'; evi#25). If China exports deflation across a fragmenting trade order, the 'capital bid for physical scarcity → elevated r*' story is at best regional — and the global macro frame that drives F8 and the duration trade is undercut. Separately, F15 (US–China goods trade ≥80% of 2024 levels in 2028) is in direct tension with the 'two loosely-coupled stacks, no stable middle' claim: 80% persistence is a great deal of coupling for a world allegedly bifurcating. The essay hand-waves ('interdependence persists — it does not defuse the tail') without reconciling high goods interdependence with claimed compute-stack separation.
top_critiques.target: Completeness of the mercantilism×AI macro frame; F15 (P=0.62) vs. the two-stack thesis (T4).
top_critiques.best_rebuttal: Bifurcation is explicitly scoped to the COMPUTE stack, not all goods — AI stacks can split while commodity and consumer-goods trade persists (that is exactly what F15 asserts). And the deflationary channel is partly acknowledged via commoditization (F2/D2), which is itself deflationary within AI.
top_critiques.remaining_vulnerability: If goods trade holds at 80%, the 'prisoner's dilemma forces redundant two-stack buildout' claim (T1 evi#39) is weaker than presented — real bifurcation should erode goods trade further. More damaging: the deflationary-East channel could flip the SIGN of r* and repression for the global system, and the thesis never prices that inversion — it simply assumes the inflationary-West case is the world case.

coherence_tensions.tension: The thesis attributes the expiry of export controls specifically to the space-compute modality (Starlink/Colossus) routing compute around the paradigm. But F5 posits that controls already fail through mundane smuggling of 100k+ restricted accelerators. These are two competing failure mechanisms: if F5 holds at 0.65, containment is already porous via a boring channel, which undercuts the thesis's framing of space-compute as THE decisive wildcard with a 'built-in expiry date.' The story implies controls are effective-until-space-compute, while F5 implies they are leaky now.
coherence_tensions.resolution: State explicitly that the two mechanisms are complementary and sequenced: smuggling (F5) degrades controls at the margin now, while space-compute (thesis) makes them structurally unenforceable later. Reframe the thesis claim as 'export controls already leak and space-compute removes the last enforcement chokepoint,' rather than presenting space-compute as the sole cause of expiry.

coherence_tensions.tension: The thesis asserts the geopolitical container 'fragments into two compute stacks with no stable middle,' yet F15 (P=0.62) forecasts 2028 US-China two-way goods trade holding at >=80% of 2024 levels — a picture of continuity, not decoupling. Taken together the forecasts tell an ambiguous story about how bifurcated the world actually becomes.
coherence_tensions.resolution: Distinguish compute/tech-stack decoupling (the thesis's actual claim) from aggregate goods trade. Clarify that 'two compute stacks' is about AI/silicon jurisdiction, and that broad bilateral goods trade can persist (F15) even as the compute layer bifurcates. Make F15's high trade level explicitly consistent with a narrow, compute-focused fragmentation.

coherence_tensions.tension: The thesis and F2 (P=0.88) strongly assert that the model layer commoditizes and rent leaks up-stack, which should erode the standalone economics of independent frontier labs. Yet F10 — an independent top-tier lab being acquired/absorbed by a platform or power controller, the natural consequence of that mechanism — is only P=0.50. The near-certain premise (commoditization) is paired with a barely-better-than-coinflip consequence.
coherence_tensions.resolution: Either raise F10 to reflect the strength of the commoditization thesis (labs lose independent rent and get absorbed by platform/power holders), or add a countervailing factor (mission-lock, regulatory blocks on acquisition, sovereign-backing keeping labs independent) that justifies keeping F10 at 0.50 despite F2.

coherence_tensions.tension: F7 forecasts measurable white-collar job displacement (implying AI is effective and adopted fast enough to cut headcount), while the macro thesis depends on productivity diffusing 'too slowly to outrun the debt.' A world where AI is already displacing workers sits in tension with a world where productivity gains are too slow to matter macro-economically.
coherence_tensions.resolution: Explicitly separate labor displacement from measured aggregate productivity: AI can eliminate specific roles (F7) without producing economy-wide TFP gains fast enough to change the debt trajectory. State that 'displacement without diffusion' is the intended reconciliation, not a contradiction.

coherence_tensions.tension: The thesis presents fiscal dominance and financial repression as the regime the macro system 'slides into' — a stated destination. But F8 (US real policy rate negative for 6+ consecutive months) is a mild, near-inevitable symptom of that regime and is priced at only 0.55, under-committed relative to how confidently the thesis asserts the repression outcome.
coherence_tensions.resolution: Either raise F8 to match the thesis's stated confidence in financial repression, or soften the thesis language from 'slides into fiscal dominance' to a more conditional formulation consistent with a ~0.55 probability. Align the strength of the narrative claim with the forecast probability.

coherence_tensions.tension: The thesis claims 'no stable middle' between the two compute stacks, which implies swing states are forced to bifurcate decisively. Yet F12 puts only P=0.45 on merely 3 of 20 swing states adopting a predominantly Chinese stack. A strong 'no stable middle' bifurcation should push adoption on one side or the other higher than a sub-coinflip on a low threshold.
coherence_tensions.resolution: Either raise F12 to reflect forced bifurcation, or reconcile by noting 'no stable middle' refers to the absence of a neutral third stack (not to speed of alignment), so swing states remain contested/unaligned for the forecast window while still ultimately lacking a middle option.

overall_verdict: This is a genuinely differentiated, well-scaffolded thesis: it reframes the AI race around physical constraints (grid, fabs, jurisdiction) and up-stack rent migration, and backs the narrative with falsifiable, probability-tagged forecasts (F1–F15) tied to explicit dimensions (D1–D5), which is exactly the falsifiable-conviction structure a Bridgewater submission rewards. However, the 6 unresolved coherence tensions and 12 missing angles are load-bearing — most notably the internal strain between "commoditization/no durable moat" and "rent captured up-stack," the space-compute wildcard (F4) doing heavy lifting at only P=0.62, and thin causal linkage between the mercantilist-capex claim and the elevated-r*/financial-repression chain. Tighten those cross-claim contradictions and shore up the weakest-probability pillars before submission.
